posts.name: David Strange
posts.cooked:
Afternoon all,

I was flicking through my list in Cellar Tracker and I was surprised that I owned some vanishingly unpopular… no… perhaps ‘obscure’ would be a better word, wines. Below I have listed those wines where the total community holdings are less than 10 bottles. As you can see, my modest collection is largely an assortment of interesting bottles rather than cases of really flash stuff.


Wine: My bottles/Total community bottles
2005 Domaine Alain Michelot Nuits St. Georges 1er Cru Aux Chaignots 1/2
2004 Domaine Arlaud Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru Les Sentiers 1/10
2008 Domaine Arlaud Clos St. Denis 1/4 (and that one is a mag… ho ho ho)
2009 Domaine Francois Bertheau Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru Les Charmes 1/1 (also a mag, I love big ones)
1996 Billecart-Salmon Champagne Brut Blanc de Blancs Grand Cru 1/2
2002 Domaine Chandon de Briailles Corton-Le Charlemagne 1/3
2008 Domaine du Colombier Hermitage 2/3
2008 Domaine du Colombier Hermitage Blanc 1/4
2006 Domaine du Colombier Hermitage Blanc 1/4
2006 J. Confuron-Cotetidot Clos Vougeot 1/7
2005 Dujac Fils et Père Nuits St. Georges 1er Cru Aux Thorey 1/2 (another mag)
1998 Germano Ettore Barolo Prapò 1/4
1997 Fritz Haag Brauneberger Juffer-Sonnenuhr Riesling Auslese #10 1/6
2005 Karthäuserhof Eitelsbacher Karthäuserhofberg Riesling Auslese #31 1/2
2006 Domaine Rossignol Trapet Chapelle-Chambertin 1/5
1999 Wwe. Dr. H. Thanisch (Erben-Thanisch) Bernkasteler Doctor Riesling Auslese 1/3
1973 Trimbach Riesling Clos Ste. Hune Cuvée Exceptionelle 1/1
2002 Domaine Vacheron Sancerre Rouge Belle Dame 1/8
2002 Van Volxem Scharzhofberger Riesling Pergentsknopp 1/7
2002 Van Volxem Wiltinger Gottesfuss Riesling Alte Reben 1/3 (and my carer has the other 2 )

For completeness, my wine most widely owned wine is:
1998 Château de Beaucastel 1/8465 (they make one hell of a lot of wine, all right)

I was rather surprised by some of these. Sure, white Hermitage and 73 CSH might be rare as hen’s teeth, but some of those I thought would be more widely owned.

I’d be intrigued to know what wines other people own that the wider Cellar Tracker-using world seems to have passed by. If the mood takes you then do share!

Cheers,
David.

posts.name: William Segui (high quality ITB)
posts.cooked:
1967 Ste. Michelle White Oak Cabernet Sauvignon - Washington, CA

It’s the oldest bottle I own, the curiousity probably out weighs any drinking pleasure it may provide - although rumor has it that Andre Tchelistcheff made/consulted on these old wines – so who knows.

posts.name: Rick Gregory
posts.cooked:
Hmm… I should take a look, that would be an interesting report to run. But in the meantime… that 73 CSH is insanely tasty. mmmmm

Will - that’s close to the first vintage CSM made. In fact, it might be the first vintage. INteresting bottle to have.

posts.name: David Strange
posts.cooked:
Rick Gregory:

Hmm… I should take a look, that would be an interesting report to run. But in the meantime… that 73 CSH is insanely tasty. mmmmm

Will - that’s close to the first vintage CSM made. In fact, it might be the first vintage. INteresting bottle to have.

Hi Rick,

It is my only birth-year wine (sadly only a good vintage for CSH, Cornas and opinionated loonies). I popped a bottle on my 30th and was amazed by its crystalline purity and coruscatingly intense expression of minerality - a stunning wine experience that wowed all my wine chums. I look forward to popping my remaining bottle when I reach the next significant age milepost.

Cheers,
David.

PS. Oh I suppose the Pol Roger 1973 I had for the 30th was rather spiffy, but it fell apart within about 4 minutes of pouring.

posts.cooked:

Look to Rioja in '73. You’ll be very pleasantly surprised.

Cheers,

posts.name: Eric LeVine
posts.cooked:
bretrooks:
Loren Sonkin:

How do you generate this list on CT?

I have a very small collection (less than 100 bottles), so I did it manually, using the cellar view’s side panel in the CT Beta.

Correct, right now I don’t include the global quantity as a column in the cellar view of an individual user, so there is no way to sort on it for now. Sorry. Longer term that is something I can add (the data is already calculated and sitting in a table that is joined to that report), but my main concern would be user confusion.

posts.name: David Strange
posts.cooked:
Les Huisman:
Jay Miller:
David Strange:

Cheers Brad, I’ll keep my eyes open for some.

Brad is definitely correct on Rioja, but I also like CA Cabernet from 1973. And the one Chablis I’ve had (a Drouhin ‘Clos’) was wonderful however I have no idea how typical that might be.

I second the 1973 CA Cabs. '73 is my birth year also, and I opened a Chateau Montelena on my 35th. It was surprisingly fresh and vibrant. Delicious stuff.

Hi chaps,

Thanks for the heads up. I haven’t had many 70s CA Cabs, but those few that have made it over the pond and into my greasy mitts seemed to me to be more sedate and refined than those of the currently extant idiom. I’ve been taken by their lower alcohol levels and ripe (not over-ripe), restrained fruit characters. A bottle of 1970 Mondavi Reserve (I seem to recall it was the Reserve, but with advancing years my memory cannot always be relied upon) I had a couple of years ago was delightfully elegant. Is this a fair assessment or just a product of my limited tasting exposure?

The only problem I have with scoring some is that they are beyond vanishingly rare here in Blighty. If someone could recommend a shop that would be willing to post a bottle of a good, but not frighteningly pricey, example by airmail I’d be terribly grateful.

Thanks again for pointing me in this direction.

Cheers,
David.

posts.name: maureen nelson
posts.cooked:
Vincent Fritzsche:

Nobody has the 1978 Taltarni Shiraz in Cellartracker, but it’s the oldest wine currently in my cellar. How’s that for obscure?

I used to own it but I drank it a long time ago.

And, David - 09 Bertheau hasn’t been offered here yet I don’t think.

Re: Claude’s comment - I suspect he means because people misidentify the wine when creating inventory - I find erroneously listed wines all the time (just ask Eric, I bug the bejeesus out of him).

posts.name: Claude Kolm
posts.cooked:
maureen nelson:

Re: Claude’s comment - I suspect he means because people misidentify the wine when creating inventory - I find erroneously listed wines all the time (just ask Eric, I bug the bejeesus out of him).

No, I’m referring to some entries for wines from vineyards and owners before that owner acquired the vineyard; wines that were never issued in the recorded vintage because of hail, freeze, poor quality, etc.; and some very famous wines that originally were produced in tiny quantities many, many years ago and so likely there are not any existing genuine bottles but nevertheless there are sales offers on the internet for them all the time (often in large formats). Fakes, in other words.

posts.name: Ken Vastola
posts.cooked:
Claude Kolm:
maureen nelson:

Re: Claude’s comment - I suspect he means because people misidentify the wine when creating inventory - I find erroneously listed wines all the time (just ask Eric, I bug the bejeesus out of him).

No, I’m referring to some entries for wines from vineyards and owners before that owner acquired the vineyard; wines that were never issued in the recorded vintage because of hail, freeze, poor quality, etc.; and some very famous wines that originally were produced in tiny quantities many, many years ago and so likely there are not any existing genuine bottles but nevertheless there are sales offers on the internet for them all the time (often in large formats). Fakes, in other words.

If you’re referring to Vincent’s comment, I assumed he meant that he owned a bottle, but does not have his wines in CT, but no one in CT has this wine.

But I could be wrong.

posts.name: Claude Kolm
posts.cooked:
Ken V:
Claude Kolm:
maureen nelson:

Re: Claude’s comment - I suspect he means because people misidentify the wine when creating inventory - I find erroneously listed wines all the time (just ask Eric, I bug the bejeesus out of him).

No, I’m referring to some entries for wines from vineyards and owners before that owner acquired the vineyard; wines that were never issued in the recorded vintage because of hail, freeze, poor quality, etc.; and some very famous wines that originally were produced in tiny quantities many, many years ago and so likely there are not any existing genuine bottles but nevertheless there are sales offers on the internet for them all the time (often in large formats). Fakes, in other words.

If you’re referring to Vincent’s comment, I assumed he meant that he owned a bottle, but does not have his wines in CT, but no one in CT has this wine.

But I could be wrong.

Not referring to it at all, and don’t know why you thought I was. I’m talking about much rarer, much more famous, much older wines that people today pay tens of thousands of dollars for, thinking they’re getting the genuine article.

posts.name: Vincent Fritzsche
posts.cooked:
For what it’s worth, no, I don’t use Cellartracker but was looking there to see if anyone had stock of '78 Taltarni Shiraz. No, but it is listed. I presume someone had it, but then consumed it.

But Claude brings up an interesting point - there are things in there that have never existed. So obviously people input things they don’t have, yet it becomes a record of some kind of validation of authenticity. The notion that if it’s in Cellartracker, perhaps it exists, even when it doesn’t. I imagine it’s impossible for CT to stay on top of what’s legit and what isn’t. But the notion of nonexistent wines being listed there seems unfortunate at best.

posts.name: Ken Vastola
posts.cooked:
Vincent Fritzsche:

But Claude brings up an interesting point - there are things in there that have never existed. So obviously people input things they don’t have, yet it becomes a record of some kind of validation of authenticity. The notion that if it’s in Cellartracker, perhaps it exists, even when it doesn’t. I imagine it’s impossible for CT to stay on top of what’s legit and what isn’t. But the notion of nonexistent wines being listed there seems unfortunate at best.

I know that Eric works very hard to eliminate these, but it is a huge task. I try to help out in areas that I follow in detail, e.g. Giacosa and other Piedmont producers. The labels are often confusing, and people often aren’t that careful, perhaps because they are entering wines while drinking them?

I’ve been guilty of this too. Recently I went to enter a TN on a 2007 Ojai Sauvignon Blanc Westerly Vineyard. I just happened to notice that I had the only 2 bottles ever put into CT. I thought this was odd. When I looked more carefully, I saw that Ojai switched their SB vineyard from Westerly to McGinley in 2007. When I bought the wine from the winery, I had just used my entry from the 2006, not realizing it was a different vineyard.

posts.name: Graeme Gee
posts.cooked:
Ken V:

I know that Eric works very hard to eliminate these, but it is a huge task. I try to help out in areas that I follow in detail, e.g. Giacosa and other Piedmont producers. The labels are often confusing, and people often aren’t that careful, perhaps because they are entering wines while drinking them?

I do the same. Wineries skip vintages, regions change; all kind of things go on. If I can substantiate an error claim, I do so and send off a note.
If a wine has no stock, no drinking/consumption history, no photo, it’s just the ‘shell’ of an entry, then it’s far more suspicious, obviously.
cheers,
GG

the 1998 Bencesbor Telkihegyi Kiralyleanyka is an Hungarian wine produced many years ago from a Budapest wine yard.

Thank you Roberto.
I still have it. Guess I’ll have to open it one of these days. By the way, the label is actually printed on leather. I had a Hungarian friend translate some of the subtext on the label a few years ago and it states the wine is from crystalline granite soils. It is also related to the Hungarian military somehow.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
I take it obscure in this context includes unicorn-type wines, which I see as wines that are relatively rare but well known (like the ‘88 Roumier BM VV)?

In that category (although that Roumier is particularly unique) I have a few but nothing super-obscure. Among them: ‘89 and ‘95 Azay-Le-Rideau Sec from Robert Denis, ‘90 von Schubert M-G Herrenberg Auslese Cask 92, ‘95 Juge Cuvee C, ‘06 Kiralyudvar Lapis, ‘89 Pinon Vouvray Réserve Passerillée (the library release just a few weeks ago), ‘89 Bourillon D’Orleans Vouvray La Coulee d’Or Tris de Nobles Grain Moelleux, ‘96 and ‘97 Domaine Petits Quarts Bonnezeaux Le Malabe 1er Trie, ‘01 Trimbach CFE 375th Ann. bottling (unique but not particularly rare).
